methodology: Ranges compiled July 2026 from web research across dealer sites and price-guide references: IGS/gemsociety.org price articles, GemSelect price guides, Gem Rock Auctions listings, gemval.com retail charts, thenaturalsapphirecompany.com and naturalgemstones.com pricing charts, ajsgem.com, gemstock.org dealer listings, rubyglint.com and gemdaia.com 2025-26 price guides, and specialty sources (folkmarketgems.com for Pakistani material, Sotheby's and veerasakgems.com for pigeon-blood ruby). For each variety the low end reflects commercial-grade faceted stones, typical reflects mid good-quality retail in the 0.5-5ct range, and high reflects fine (not auction/world-class) quality; the big four (emerald, ruby, sapphire, peridot) were cross-checked against at least two independent sources each and figures rounded to avoid false precision.
disclaimer: Indicative retail ranges only - color, clarity, treatment, origin certification and size drive per-carat price far more than species, so individual stones can fall well outside these bands.

entries.qualityNote: Pale or heavily-oiled commercial stones sit at the bottom; vivid green with only minor oil triples the price, and fine Colombian color goes far beyond this band.
Commercial transparent Swat material trades at $50-250/ct, but the deep saturated slightly bluish-green top color with good clarity fetches $1,500-3,000+/ct and rivals Colombian stones.
Dark or purplish heated stones stay under $500/ct; a clean open red moves toward $2,000+/ct, and per-carat rates jump sharply above 2ct.

Premium only exists with a lab report (GIA/GRS/Gubelin) stating no heat and pigeon-blood color; Burmese origin and sizes over 1ct multiply the rate, with sub-1ct Burmese stones alone at $15,000-40,000/ct.
Very dark or greenish blues stay commercial; a velvety royal/cornflower blue at 1ct brings $800-1,200/ct and roughly doubles per carat at 2ct.
Saturation is everything - pale lemon stones sit near $50-150/ct while vivid golden-yellow Ceylon stones over 2ct reach $1,000-1,600/ct.
Grayish or overly dark purples stay commercial; a vivid medium violet-purple, especially unheated, pushes past $750/ct and fine stones well beyond.
Commercial dark greens open at $25-150/ct; open bluish-green teal color without a closed dark axis is what carries stones to $300-600/ct.
True blue without heavy gray or over-darkness is rare; clean saturated blue over 1-2ct is what moves stones from $100-300/ct to $600-1,000/ct.
Pure red to raspberry-red with acceptable clarity commands the top; brownish or heavily included pink-reds fall to commercial levels.
Schorl is abundant, so value is mostly cutting labor and finish; only intricate custom cuts or fine crystal specimens exceed $30/ct.
Brownish-orange stones stay near the bottom; the pure vivid 'Fanta' mandarin orange with eye-clean clarity is what reaches $500-1,500/ct.
Raspberry purplish-red without over-darkness drives value; clean stones over 3ct with fine color climb toward $150-300/ct.
Cinnamon-orange with good transparency tops out around $200/ct; the typical treacly, heavily-included material stays cheap.
Yellowish-green commercial material is $15-50/ct; a clean vivid green in 1-3ct sizes brings $50-100/ct, and per-carat rates roughly triple by 5-10ct.
Supat Valley stones with pure grass-green color and no brown carry a strong origin premium; large 10ct+ vivid stones reach $300-900/ct.
Irradiated blue topaz is one of the cheapest fine gems; only large sizes and precision cutting lift the rate.
The deepest treated blue commands the top of the blue-topaz market; fancy cuts and 5ct+ sizes push toward $60-120/ct at retail.
Genuine untreated orange-to-pinkish 'imperial' color from Ouro Preto, Brazil is a different market entirely; the pinker and more saturated, the closer to $3,500/ct.
The deep reddish-orange Madeira color is the top of the citrine market; ordinary honey tones sit near $20-30/ct.
Pale lemon-yellow quartz is abundant; value comes almost entirely from clean material and good cutting rather than the color itself.
Deep royal purple with red/blue flashes ('Siberian' color) tops out near $100/ct; lighter lilac drops under $10/ct.
Almost all prasiolite is heat-treated amethyst; a saturated mint-green in large clean sizes is the only thing that lifts it toward $50/ct.
Pale silvery-blue is plentiful under $100/ct; saturated 'Santa Maria' deep blue, especially over 3ct, brings $300-900/ct.
Almost always untreated, which supports strong prices; vivid hot pink (Mahenge) and pure red over 1ct escalate fast, with fine reds exceeding $5,000-8,000/ct.
Value hinges on a sharp 50/50 purple-yellow zone boundary with strong color on both sides; per-carat price barely rises with size.
Too soft for jewelry, so it prices as a collector stone; rare colors (pink, color-change) and large precision-faceted clean stones reach $50-100/ct.
Abundant in large clean sizes, so price is nearly all cutting quality; precision or fantasy cuts fetch $10-25/ct while ordinary goods sit near $3-5/ct.
